content.problemStatement:
You are given a list of integers heightsheightsheights representing the heights of NNN mountain peaks. For each peak, you need to find the height of the next higher peak that comes after it in the list. If there is no higher peak after a given peak, return −1-1−1 for that peak.


Input Format

The first line contains one integer nnn, the size of the list of integers.                                                                                                               - Next line contains nnn integers heights[0],heights[1]...heights[n]heights[0],heights[1]...heights[n]heights[0],heights[1]...heights[n], representing the peaks .


Output Format

For each peak, print the next higher peak that comes after it in the list. If there is no higher peak for a peak than print −1-1−1.
content.inputFormat: First line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case, the first line contains an integer n, the number of mountain peaks. The second line contains n space-separated integers heights[0], heights[1], ..., heights[n-1] representing the heights of the peaks.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing n space-separated integers. The i-th integer is the height of the next higher peak that appears after position i in the same test case, or -1 if no such peak exists.

sampleTestCases.explanation: There is one test case. For the first peak (1) the next higher peak is 2. For the second peak (2) there is no higher peak, so -1.
Only one test case. The first 6 has no higher peak after it. 5's next higher is the last 6. 3's next higher is also the last 6. The final 6 has none.
Strictly increasing heights, so each element's next higher is the immediate next one, and the last element gets -1.

solution.approach: Traverse the array from left to right while maintaining a stack of indices whose next greater element has not been found yet. For each new height h[i], pop indices from the stack while h[i] is greater than the height at those indices; for each popped index, h[i] is the answer. Then push i onto the stack. After the scan, any index left in the stack has no greater element to its right, so its answer stays -1. This monotonic-stack technique runs in O(n) time per test case.
solution.timeComplexity: O(n) per test case, O(total N) for the whole input.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(n) auxiliary space for the answer array and the stack.

code.Python3:
import sys

def solve():
    data = sys.stdin.read().strip().split()
    if not data:
        return
    it = iter(data)
    T = int(next(it))
    out_lines = []
    for _ in range(T):
        n = int(next(it))
        h = [int(next(it)) for _ in range(n)]
        ans = [-1] * n
        stack = []  # store indices
        for i, val in enumerate(h):
            while stack and val > h[stack[-1]]:
                idx = stack.pop()
                ans[idx] = val
            stack.append(i)
        out_lines.append(' '.join(map(str, ans)))
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out_lines))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    solve()
